original_abstract:
Multitask prompted finetuning (MTF) has proven highly effective at enabling large language models to generalize to unseen tasks in zero-shot settings. However, research in this area has primarily focused on English-only models and datasets. In this work, we apply MTF to the multilingual BLOOM and mT5 model families, resulting in two new sets of finetuned models: BLOOMZ and mT0.

Our experiments demonstrate that finetuning multilingual models on English tasks with English prompts significantly enhances their ability to generalize to non-English tasks, even when those languages were only encountered during pretraining. By extending the training mixture to include multilingual tasks and machine-translated prompts—a collection we introduce as xP3—we achieve further performance gains and establish new state-of-the-art results in zero-shot crosslingual benchmarks. 

Remarkably, we observe that these models can generalize to tasks in languages that were not intentionally included in either pretraining or finetuning, suggesting the acquisition of higher-level, language-agnostic capabilities. We conclude that multitask finetuning on a diverse range of languages and tasks is a robust strategy for developing capable multilingual assistants. Our code, datasets, and models are made publicly available.
humanized_abstract:
Multitask prompted finetuning (MTF) has demonstrated strong ability of multi-purpose large language models to learn to apply their capabilities to novel tasks in a zero-shot fashion. The research on MTF has mainly focused on English-only models and datasets. In this work, we apply MTF to multilingual BLOOM and mT5 model families and obtain two new sets of finetuned models, namely BLOOMZ and mT0.

Finetuning multilingual language models on English data with English prompts results in strong cross-language generalization on unseen test sets in other languages, even if those languages were never seen at training time. Adding the multilingual tasks and machine-translated prompts from the xP3 training set further improves the performance on the zero-shot cross-lingual benchmarks.

Surprisingly, we find that these models are also able to perform tasks in unseen languages (i.e. languages not present in the training or finetuning data) suggesting they have learned a set of general skills not specific to particular languages. We therefore conclude that multitask finetuning over a diverse set of languages and tasks is an effective way to build a multilingual personal assistant. Code, datasets and models released.

document.output:
Multitask prompted finetuning (MTF) has demonstrated strong ability of multi-purpose large language models to learn to apply their capabilities to novel tasks in a zero-shot fashion. The research on MTF has mainly focused on English-only models and datasets. In this work, we apply MTF to multilingual BLOOM and mT5 model families and obtain two new sets of finetuned models, namely BLOOMZ and mT0.

Finetuning multilingual language models on English data with English prompts results in strong cross-language generalization on unseen test sets in other languages, even if those languages were never seen at training time. Adding the multilingual tasks and machine-translated prompts from the xP3 training set further improves the performance on the zero-shot cross-lingual benchmarks.

Surprisingly, we find that these models are also able to perform tasks in unseen languages (i.e. languages not present in the training or finetuning data) suggesting they have learned a set of general skills not specific to particular languages. We therefore conclude that multitask finetuning over a diverse set of languages and tasks is an effective way to build a multilingual personal assistant. Code, datasets and models released.
document.input:
Multitask prompted finetuning (MTF) has proven highly effective at enabling large language models to generalize to unseen tasks in zero-shot settings. However, research in this area has primarily focused on English-only models and datasets. Remarkably, we observe that these models can generalize to tasks in languages that were not intentionally included in either pretraining or finetuning, suggesting the acquisition of higher-level, language-agnostic capabilities.
